architecture.summary: egg is a library implementing e-graphs (equality graphs) for equality saturation. An e-graph compactly represents many equivalent expressions as equivalence classes (e-classes) with congruence relations between them. The system reads in a program/expression, applies rewrite rules that add equivalent expressions to the e-graph, and saturates the graph until no new rewrites apply. Two key mechanisms: (1) rebuilding, an amortized invariant restoration technique that efficiently maintains congruence after rule application, and (2) e-class analyses, a domain-specific analysis framework that attaches analyses to e-classes to filter and guide rewrites. Information flows via rule matching over e-graph nodes, rule application adding new e-nodes, and periodic rebuilding to restore parent-child congruence invariants.

biblio.title: egg: Fast and extensible equality saturation
biblio.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Programming Languages

claims.statement: A new amortized invariant restoration technique called rebuilding takes advantage of equality saturation's distinct workload, providing asymptotic speedups over current techniques in practice.
A general mechanism called e-class analyses integrates domain-specific analyses into the e-graph, reducing the need for ad hoc manipulation.
egg's case studies on three previously published applications of equality saturation highlight how egg's performance and flexibility enable state-of-the-art results across diverse domains.
